---
title: "Ch 12b — Derivatives (Introduction)"
subtitle: "First principles and basic rules: the slope of a function at a single point"
lesson-id: "cbse11.derivatives-intro"
tier: "cbse11"
ncert-class: 11
ncert-chapter: 12
ncert-chapter-title: "Limits and Derivatives"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse11.limits"]
unlocks: ["cbse12.continuity", "cbse12.application-derivatives"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you compute simple limits from Lesson 12a?
2. Are you fluent with the slope formula
   $(y_2 - y_1)/(x_2 - x_1)$?
3. Do you know $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^n)$ informally as $n x^{n-1}$, even
   without proof?
4. Can you state $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin x}{x} = 1$?

Three confident yeses — read on. Otherwise revisit
**[Limits](../12a-limits/index.qmd)**.
:::

## Touch

A car drives along a straight road. Its position at time $t$ seconds is
$s(t) = t^2$ metres.

- Average speed from $t = 1$ to $t = 3$: $(9 - 1)/(3 - 1) = 4$ m/s.
- From $t = 1$ to $t = 1.5$: $(2.25 - 1)/0.5 = 2.5$ m/s.
- From $t = 1$ to $t = 1.01$: $(1.0201 - 1)/0.01 = 2.01$ m/s.
- From $t = 1$ to $t = 1.0001$: ≈ $2.0001$ m/s.

The values converge to $2$ — the **instantaneous speed** at $t = 1$.

The widget below shrinks the time interval and shows the average speed
approaching the slope of the tangent line.

```{ojs}
viewof hVal = Inputs.range([0.001, 1], {value: 0.5, step: 0.001, label: "h (interval width)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const t = 1, h = hVal;
  const s = t => t * t;
  const avg = (s(t + h) - s(t)) / h;
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;line-height:1.7;">
    <div><b>s(t) = t²</b>, evaluating at t = 1, h = ${h.toFixed(4)}.</div>
    <div>Average rate: (s(1+h) − s(1))/h = <b>${avg.toFixed(6)}</b></div>
    <div style="margin-top:0.4em;color:#666;">
      As h → 0, this approaches <b>2</b> = ds/dt at t = 1.
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

The instantaneous rate of change is *the limit* of the average rate.
That's the derivative.

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. Why does the average rate become the *instantaneous* rate as
   $h \to 0$? What if $h = 0$ outright?
2. The derivative of $x^2$ at $x = 1$ is $2$. At $x = 3$ it is $6$.
   Pattern?
3. The graph of $f(x) = |x|$ has a sharp corner at $x = 0$. Does the
   derivative exist there?
4. If $g(x) = c$ (constant), what is $g'(x)$? Why?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(2) is the **power rule**: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^n) = n x^{n-1}$. (3) is the
canonical example of a function that is continuous but not
differentiable.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — From first principles

Compute $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = x^2$ from the definition
$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \dfrac{f(x + h) - f(x)}{h}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$f(x + h) - f(x) = (x + h)^2 - x^2 = 2xh + h^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{2xh + h^2}{h} = 2x + h \to 2x$ as $h \to 0$.

So $f'(x) = 2x$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Constant

Compute $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = 5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$f(x + h) - f(x) = 5 - 5 = 0$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{0}{h} = 0 \to 0$. So $f'(x) = 0$ for any constant $f$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Linear

Compute $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = 3x + 7$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$f(x + h) - f(x) = 3h$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{3h}{h} = 3 \to 3$. So $f'(x) = 3$, the slope of the line.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Power rule

Compute $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = x^3$ from first principles.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$(x + h)^3 = x^3 + 3 x^2 h + 3 x h^2 + h^3$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{3 x^2 h + 3 x h^2 + h^3}{h} = 3 x^2 + 3 x h + h^2 \to 3 x^2$.

So $f'(x) = 3 x^2$. Pattern: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^n) = n x^{n-1}$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Sum and difference rules

Differentiate $f(x) = 2x^3 - 5x^2 + 7x - 4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Differentiate term by term using the power rule.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f'(x) = 6 x^2 - 10 x + 7$.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Product rule

Use the product rule $(uv)' = u' v + u v'$ to differentiate
$f(x) = (x^2)(x + 1)$. Verify by expanding first.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
By product rule: $f'(x) = 2x \cdot (x + 1) + x^2 \cdot 1 = 2 x^2 + 2x + x^2 = 3 x^2 + 2x$.

By expanding: $f(x) = x^3 + x^2 \Rightarrow f'(x) = 3 x^2 + 2x$. ✓
</details>

### Problem 7 — Trig derivative

From first principles, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin x)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use $\sin(x + h) - \sin x = 2 \cos(x + h/2) \sin(h/2)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{\sin(x + h) - \sin x}{h} = \dfrac{2 \cos(x + h/2) \sin(h/2)}{h}
= \cos(x + h/2) \cdot \dfrac{\sin(h/2)}{h/2}$.

As $h \to 0$: $\cos(x + 0) \cdot 1 = \cos x$.

So $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin x) = \cos x$.
</details>

You have just used **first principles**, the **power rule**, the **sum
rule**, the **product rule**, and the **derivative of $\sin$**. Now we
name them.

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

The **derivative** of $f$ at $x$:
$$ f'(x) = \dfrac{df}{dx} = \lim_{h \to 0} \dfrac{f(x + h) - f(x)}{h}, $$
provided the limit exists.

**Geometric interpretation:** the slope of the tangent line to $y = f(x)$
at the point $(x, f(x))$.

**Standard derivatives:**

| $f(x)$ | $f'(x)$ |
|:--|:--|
| $c$ (constant) | $0$ |
| $x^n$ | $n x^{n-1}$ |
| $\sin x$ | $\cos x$ |
| $\cos x$ | $-\sin x$ |
| $\tan x$ | $\sec^2 x$ |
| $\sec x$ | $\sec x \tan x$ |

**Rules of differentiation:**

- **Sum:** $(f \pm g)' = f' \pm g'$.
- **Constant multiple:** $(c f)' = c f'$.
- **Product:** $(uv)' = u' v + u v'$.
- **Quotient:** $\left(\dfrac{u}{v}\right)' = \dfrac{u' v - u v'}{v^2}$.

(The **chain rule** is reserved for Class 12.)

:::

A short verse:

> *Slope of the tangent — that is the derivative;*
> *Limit of $h \to 0$ — that is the conservative.*
> *Power becomes coefficient, exponent shifts down;*
> *And constants vanish as the rate is found.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Differentiate $f(x) = (3 x^2 + 1)(2x - 5)$ using the product rule.

How a competent solver thinks:

1. Let $u = 3 x^2 + 1$, $v = 2x - 5$. Then $u' = 6x$, $v' = 2$.
2. $(uv)' = u' v + u v' = 6x (2x - 5) + (3 x^2 + 1)(2)$
   $= 12 x^2 - 30 x + 6 x^2 + 2 = 18 x^2 - 30 x + 2$.
3. (Verify by expanding first: $f(x) = 6 x^3 - 15 x^2 + 2x - 5$,
   $f'(x) = 18 x^2 - 30 x + 2$. ✓)

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Differentiate $f(x) = x^7$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$7 x^6$.
</details>

**W2.** Differentiate $f(x) = 4 x^5 - 3 x^2 + 6$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$20 x^4 - 6x$.
</details>

**W3.** $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\cos x)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-\sin x$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Differentiate $\sqrt{x}$ from first principles.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Rationalise: $\sqrt{x + h} - \sqrt{x} = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{\sqrt{x+h} - \sqrt{x}}{h} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{x+h} + \sqrt{x}}
\to \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$.

So $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^{1/2}) = \dfrac{1}{2} x^{-1/2}$ — consistent with
the power rule.
</details>

**P2.** Differentiate $f(x) = \dfrac{x + 1}{x - 1}$ using the quotient rule.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$u = x + 1, v = x - 1$. $u' = 1, v' = 1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f'(x) = \dfrac{1 \cdot (x - 1) - (x + 1) \cdot 1}{(x - 1)^2} = \dfrac{-2}{(x - 1)^2}$.
</details>

**P3.** Differentiate $f(x) = x^2 \sin x$ at $x = \pi/2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Product rule.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f'(x) = 2x \sin x + x^2 \cos x$.

At $x = \pi/2$: $2 \cdot \pi/2 \cdot 1 + (\pi/2)^2 \cdot 0 = \pi$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** From first principles, find $f'(x)$ for $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{f(x + h) - f(x)}{h} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{x+h} - \dfrac{1}{x}}{h}
= \dfrac{x - (x + h)}{x(x + h) \cdot h} = \dfrac{-1}{x(x + h)}$.

As $h \to 0$: $-\dfrac{1}{x^2}$. Consistent with $x^{-1}$ → $-x^{-2}$.
</details>

**S2.** Find the equation of the tangent line to $y = x^2 - 4x + 5$ at
$x = 3$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Tangent slope = $f'(3)$. Point: $(3, f(3))$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f(3) = 9 - 12 + 5 = 2$. $f'(x) = 2x - 4$, so $f'(3) = 2$.

Tangent: $y - 2 = 2(x - 3) \Rightarrow y = 2x - 4$.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^{100})$ at $x = 1$ equals
(a) $100$ (b) $99$ (c) $1$ (d) $0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$100 x^{99}$ at $x = 1$ is $100$. **Answer: (a).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Differentiate $f(x) = (2x + 3)^2$ by
expanding and applying the power rule.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f(x) = 4 x^2 + 12 x + 9$. $f'(x) = 8x + 12$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** From first principles, find the
derivative of $f(x) = \cos x$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\cos(x + h) - \cos x = -2 \sin(x + h/2) \sin(h/2)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{\cos(x + h) - \cos x}{h} = \dfrac{-2 \sin(x + h/2) \sin(h/2)}{h}
= -\sin(x + h/2) \cdot \dfrac{\sin(h/2)}{h/2}$.

As $h \to 0$: $-\sin x \cdot 1 = -\sin x$.

So $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\cos x) = -\sin x$.
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** Differentiate $f(x) = \dfrac{x \sin x}{1 + \cos x}$ at $x = \pi/2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Quotient rule with $u = x \sin x$ (use product rule for $u'$) and
$v = 1 + \cos x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$u = x \sin x$. $u' = \sin x + x \cos x$.

$v = 1 + \cos x$. $v' = -\sin x$.

$f'(x) = \dfrac{(\sin x + x \cos x)(1 + \cos x) - (x \sin x)(-\sin x)}{(1 + \cos x)^2}$

$= \dfrac{(\sin x + x \cos x)(1 + \cos x) + x \sin^2 x}{(1 + \cos x)^2}$.

At $x = \pi/2$: $\sin = 1, \cos = 0$. Numerator: $(1 + (\pi/2) \cdot 0)(1 + 0) + (\pi/2)(1) = 1 + \pi/2$.
Denominator: $1$.

$f'(\pi/2) = 1 + \pi/2$.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Ch 12 LA "Differentiate
$\frac{x \sin x}{1 + \cos x}$" matches the 5-mark item.
**JEE pointer:** first-principles derivative of $\sin x, \cos x$ is
JEE Main standard.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$f'(a) = f(a)/a$ in some way."** No. The derivative is the limit
   of a *difference quotient*, not the value at a point.
2. **"Power rule for $x^x$."** Doesn't apply — the exponent is itself
   variable, requiring logarithmic differentiation (Class 12).
3. **"$|x|$ is differentiable everywhere."** It is not — the corner at
   $x = 0$ has different left and right slopes ($-1$ and $+1$).
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you compute $f'(x)$ from first principles for $f(x) = x^n$,
   $\sin x$, $\cos x$ on demand?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 7 *Discover* problems and 5 of the 7
   practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Can you apply the product and quotient rules confidently?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Statistics](../13-statistics/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Stretch S2 and the 5-mark item.
- **0–1 yes:** drill first principles on five fresh functions.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Limits](../12a-limits/index.qmd)** — the derivative is a limit.

### What this unlocks

- **[Class 12 — Continuity & Differentiability](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/04-continuity/index.qmd)** —
  rigorous study of when derivatives exist.
- **[Class 12 — Application of Derivatives](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/06-application-derivatives/index.qmd)** —
  maxima/minima, rate problems.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 11, Chapter 12 — Limits and Derivatives** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised). Sections 12.4–12.5: derivative from first principles,
  algebra of derivatives.
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  [`t4.derivative`](../../tier-4-precalc-calculus/derivative/index.qmd) and
  [`t4.differentiation-rules`](../../tier-4-precalc-calculus/differentiation-rules/index.qmd).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Gradient descent updates a parameter $\theta$ by
$\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \cdot \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \theta}$.
The partial derivative is the slope of the loss surface — exactly the
derivative you just learned, applied to functions of many variables.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Velocity is the derivative of position. Acceleration
is the derivative of velocity. Every physics-engine integration step
updates position via $x \leftarrow x + v \, dt$ — one Euler step of
the inverse problem (integration).
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

Touchstones: 3Blue1Brown (the "essence of calculus" — derivative as
slope), Better Explained (instantaneous rate framing), Russian school
(Discover sequence forces first-principles derivation of the power
rule), Bhāskara (verse).
:::
